The public goods game: group of 4, each gets $10. Contributions get multiplied by 0.4 and each player receives that amount. So each $ contributed yields total return of 0.4*4 = $1.6 distributed equally? Actually each $ contributed gives each player $0.40, so total group payoff = 0.40 * 4 * total contributions = 1.6 * total contributions. So each contributed dollar yields net social benefit of +0.6 (since contributor loses $1 and gets $0.40 back, net -0.6; others each get +0.40). So free riding is dominant individually: keep all $10, get $4*0.40*others contributions plus your own? Actually if you contribute nothing, you still get 0.40 * total contributions from others. So contributions benefit all equally.

But maybe you might contribute a little to encourage reciprocity? Your "willingness to give to good causes without expecting anything in return" is average (5), but your "assume people have only the best intentions" is low, meaning you don't trust others will reciprocate. So you likely not contribute.
